sources.notes: Read the operation, both optimization objectives, and the full constraints: sum(n)<=10^6, so the final algorithm must be linear.

Official hints, optimality lemmas, and O(n) reference implementation were inspected. They establish the w=2 invariant, the nw-1 target for w>=3, unimodality of final-w last-touch times, and the bidirectional run scan.
Independent Luogu explanation was consulted for equality propagation, the treatment of terminal/internal w-runs, and the compressed-state brute checker used to debug the delicate boundary cases.

Re-derived the w=2 run invariant and the w>=3 reverse-wave model. A direct state-graph BFS was compared with a line-by-line port of the official O(n) algorithm on all 2,710 arrays for w=2,n<=7; w=3,n<=6; and w=4,n<=5, with exact agreement on both outputs.

annotation.summary: Repeatedly replace an adjacent equal pair by any unequal pair in [1,w]. Determine the largest reachable array sum and, among all ways to reach it, the minimum number of operations.
annotation.constraints: 1<=t<=10^5, 1<=n<=2*10^5, 2<=w<=10^8, sum(n)<=10^6. The sum needs 64-bit arithmetic and the intended total complexity is O(sum(n)).
annotation.core_idea: First separate immobile states: with no adjacent equality no operation is possible, and the all-w array is already optimal. For w=2, maximal runs of 1 are independent: an operation on 11 can remove exactly one 1 from its run but can never erase the run's final 1. If there are R runs and C ones, the answer is (2n-R, C-R). For w>=3, whenever an operation is available and the array is not all w, all-w is impossible because every performed pair must end unequal, while n-1 copies of w plus one w-1 are constructible; hence the maximum is nw-1. To minimize operations, view an equal pair as a movable operation token: aa followed by b can be rewritten so the equality moves one position, using a third value when w itself blocks the sweep. Reverse the process from the final single-defect array. The official last-touch lemma says the times at which the final w positions become fixed are first increasing and then decreasing, so an optimum is determined by a seed equal pair and one of the two expansion orientations. After optionally leaving terminal w-runs untouched, scan each orientation and enumerate every seed. The no-obstacle cost is the distance needed to carry the equality to the chosen end plus the return expansion. Internal maximal w-runs add a length-only penalty: 2 for a singleton and ceil(L/2) for L>=2, with one local saving when a singleton can be absorbed beside the seed. Maintaining this cumulative run penalty while scanning, and repeating on the reversed active interval, evaluates every optimal form in O(n).
annotation.complexity: O(n) time and O(1) auxiliary memory per test case; O(sum(n)) overall.
tricks: Treat an adjacent equal pair as the movable token that enables the next local rewrite.
Reverse from the unique w-1 defect and use unimodal last-touch times to reduce arbitrary operation histories to two directional sweeps.
For w=2, compress the process to independent maximal runs of 1.
For w>=3, aggregate each internal run of w by its closed-form extra cost instead of simulating operations.
Terminal w-runs may remain fixed; isolated w values next to the seed require a one-operation correction.
Run the same scan in both directions by reversing the active interval.
review_risks: The high-level optimality follows the official two lemmas, but the editorial and Luogu article do not give a fully formal proof for every boundary correction in the compact scan.
The implementation is unusually case-sensitive: single w, two consecutive w values, long terminal w-runs, and the seed immediately after an isolated w must be distinguished.
Small-state exhaustive agreement strongly supports the implementation, but it is not a substitute for a complete published proof of all scan formulas.

solution_variants.name: reverse-wave-run-scan
solution_variants.summary: Handle w=2 by counting runs of 1. For w>=3, use the reverse last-touch characterization, enumerate the seed equality and both propagation directions, and maintain closed-form penalties of encountered w-runs while allowing terminal w blocks to stay fixed.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n) time and O(1) auxiliary memory

tags.evidence: The maximum nw-1 for w>=3 is attained by explicitly propagating an equal pair and constructing n-1 values w plus one value w-1; the minimum-operation algorithm enumerates canonical constructions.
Optimality and the linear scan are obtained by reversing from the final single-defect array and analyzing the last operation times of positions that become fixed at w.
Correctness fundamentally splits into w=2 versus w>=3 and then distinguishes immobile arrays, all-w arrays, terminal w-runs, internal w-runs, and isolated-w absorption cases.
The adjacent equal pair is maintained as a reusable local buffer/token: one operation rewrites it so that a new equality appears one position farther along the chosen sweep.
Potentially long blocks of w are handled by the aggregate penalty 2 for length 1 and ceil(L/2) for length at least 2, avoiding operation-by-operation simulation.
For w=2 each initial run of 1 must retain one 1, while for w>=3 the final-w last-touch order is unimodal; these invariants provide the lower bounds matched by construction.
Leftward and rightward propagation are symmetric and are covered by running the same scan on the active interval and its reversal.
The accepted linear formula requires careful indexing and several boundary corrections, but implementation detail is not the conceptual retrieval target.
